paper.title: Gut Microbiome Wellness Index 2 enhances health status prediction from gut microbiome taxonomic profiles

proof_flows.name: Pooling and uniformly reprocessing published stool metagenomes yields a diverse training cohort with health-associated structure and a sparse Lasso feature set.

items.verify: A non-empty pooled training dataset artifact exists and is readable, containing per-sample phenotype labels and taxon-level features suitable for GMWI2 training.
items.paper_reference: Fig.1a; Methods: Multi-study pooling of human stool metagenomes
items.expected: A usable sample-level training matrix or equivalent serialized dataset for the pooled cohort is present.

items.verify: The pooled cohort size and class composition match the paper.
items.expected: The pooled dataset contains 8069 stool metagenomes: 5547 healthy and 2522 non-healthy, assembled from 54 studies across 12 phenotypes.

items.verify: The study manifest reproduces the included-study roster and study-level annotations.
items.expected: A study manifest lists the included studies with author/year, healthy vs non-healthy counts, sequencing platform, and country/geography consistent with the publication table.

items.verify: The demographic summary shows the same broad geographic and subject-distribution pattern as the paper.
items.expected: The cohort summary spans many countries and continents, shows a broad adult age distribution, and has roughly balanced known male/female labels.

items.verify: Healthy and non-healthy pooled microbiome profiles remain significantly different in the multivariate analysis.
items.expected: PERMANOVA/Adonis on pooled profiles gives R^2 = 1.2% and P = 0.001 for healthy vs non-healthy separation.

items.verify: A readable coefficient table for the trained Lasso-penalized logistic regression model exists, with taxon identifiers and signed coefficients.
items.paper_reference: Fig.2b; Methods: Estimating disease likelihood using Lasso-penalized logistic regression
items.expected: A non-empty model coefficient artifact is present and can be used to score samples.

items.verify: The sparsity pattern of the final GMWI2 model matches the paper.
items.expected: The model has 49 positive coefficients, 46 negative coefficients, and 3105 zero coefficients, for 95 non-zero coefficients out of 3200 taxa.

proof_flows.name: GMWI2 scores derived from the pooled training cohort outperform GMWI and achieve higher accuracy for high-confidence samples in cross-validation.

items.verify: Training-set, cutoff, and cross-validation metric artifacts are present and readable for GMWI2 benchmarking.
items.paper_reference: Fig.3a-e; Methods: Evaluation of classification performance
items.expected: Usable score/metric outputs exist for the training set, LOOCV, 10-fold CV, and cutoff analyses.

items.verify: Training-set discrimination matches the main result and exceeds the original GMWI baseline.
items.expected: On the training set, GMWI2 reaches balanced accuracy 79.9%, with healthy accuracy 79.2%, non-healthy accuracy 80.6%, and Cliff's Delta 0.75. The original GMWI baseline is balanced accuracy 71.8% with Cliff's Delta 0.63.

items.verify: Disease-specific distributions show the same directional separation as the paper.
items.expected: The healthy group has higher GMWI2 scores than every disease phenotype distribution shown.

items.verify: Score-bin enrichment trends follow the confidence interpretation described in the paper.
items.expected: More positive GMWI2 bins are enriched for truly healthy samples, more negative bins are enriched for truly non-healthy samples, and this trend is visually stronger than for GMWI.

items.verify: Training-set cutoff-performance tradeoff matches the reported values.
items.expected: With |GMWI2| cutoffs of 0.5 and 1.0 on the training data, balanced accuracy rises to 85.8% and 91.0%, while retained sample counts are 6364 (78.9%) and 4712 (58.4%), respectively.
items.tolerance: accuracy ±0.5 percentage points; counts ±0; retained percentages ±0.1 percentage points

items.verify: Cross-validation results reproduce the paper's main accuracy claims.
items.expected: At cutoff 0, LOOCV balanced accuracy is 79.1% (78.6% healthy, 79.5% non-healthy) and 10-fold CV balanced accuracy is 79.0% (78.6% healthy, 79.3% non-healthy). At cutoff 1.0, LOOCV and 10-fold CV balanced accuracies are 90.4% and 90.2%, respectively.

proof_flows.name: Inter-study validation shows GMWI2 generalizes across cohorts with stable held-out accuracy and AUC.

items.verify: Per-study ISV metrics and ROC outputs are produced and readable.
items.paper_reference: Fig.4a-b; Methods: Evaluation of classification performance
items.expected: Usable held-out-study performance tables and ROC data/plots exist for inter-study validation.

items.verify: Average ISV performance matches the reported robustness claim.
items.expected: Mean inter-study-validation balanced accuracy is 75.8%, and restricting to samples with GMWI2 < -1 or > 1 raises it to 86.9%.

items.verify: The held-out study scatter/bar relationship supports the same interpretation as the paper.
items.expected: Held-out-study accuracy varies across studies, but there is no clear relationship between performance and held-out study sample size.

items.verify: ROC AUC values for the three evaluation schemes match the paper.
items.expected: AUC is 0.88 on the training set, 0.87 in 10-fold CV, and 0.84 in inter-study validation.

proof_flows.name: Applying the trained GMWI2 model to independent external cohorts reproduces healthy-versus-diseased separation and cohort-level accuracy.

items.verify: A readable external-validation scoring artifact exists for independent samples with predicted GMWI2 scores and cohort labels.
items.expected: Usable external-validation sample scores and cohort annotations are present.

items.verify: Overall external validation reproduces the reported class separation and accuracy.
items.expected: The external validation set contains 1140 samples: 494 healthy and 646 non-healthy. Healthy scores are higher than non-healthy with P = 1.6 × 10^-43 (two-sided Mann-Whitney U), Cliff's Delta = 0.48, and balanced accuracy = 72.1%.
items.tolerance: counts ±0; balanced accuracy ±1.0 percentage points; Cliff's Delta ±0.05; P ±50% relative

items.verify: The reject-option cutoff improves external validation accuracy by the reported amount.
items.expected: On the external validation set, |GMWI2| cutoffs of 0.5 and 1.0 raise balanced accuracy to 75.4% and 80.1%, while retaining 74.3% and 49.3% of samples, respectively.
items.tolerance: accuracy ±1.0 percentage points; retained percentage ±1.0 percentage points

items.verify: Cohort-level score distributions show the same directional pattern as the paper.
items.expected: Healthy cohort distributions generally lie above disease cohort distributions, and the weakest healthy cohort still exceeds the pancreatic cancer cohort from the same study.

items.verify: Cohort-level classification accuracies match the paper.
items.expected: Cohort accuracies are H1 96.3% (130/135), H2 91.2% (52/57), H3 83.3% (25/30), H4 56.8% (21/37), H5 28.1% (66/235), pancreatic cancer 90.7% (39/43), Parkinson's disease 81.2% (398/490), and ankylosing spondylitis 80.5% (91/113).

proof_flows.name: In IBS fecal microbiota transplantation recipients, GMWI2 increases only in patients reporting symptom relief.

items.verify: A readable FMT longitudinal output exists with donor, baseline, and 6-month recipient GMWI2 values separated by response group.
items.expected: Usable per-sample FMT GMWI2 results are present for donors and recipients before and after transplantation.

items.verify: The FMT cohort structure and the significant response-group result match the paper.
items.expected: The IBS FMT analysis contains 22 recipients, split into 14 in the Effect group and 8 in the No Effect group, plus 17 donor samples. The Effect group shows a significant baseline-to-6-month increase with P = 0.039 (figure annotation; one-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank test).

items.verify: The response-group direction of change matches the paper.
items.expected: Only the symptom-relief group shows a clear upward shift in GMWI2 after FMT, whereas the No Effect group does not show a comparable increase.

proof_flows.name: During short-term dietary intervention, GMWI2 drops only under exclusive enteral nutrition and remains stable for vegan or omnivore diets.

items.verify: Diet-intervention time-course outputs exist for EEN, Vegan, and Omnivore groups across baseline and intervention days.
items.expected: Usable per-timepoint GMWI2 outputs are present for all diet groups.

items.verify: The diet-intervention cohort size matches the paper.
items.paper_reference: Results: In the second study26; Fig.6b
items.expected: The diet-intervention analysis uses 30 healthy volunteers.

items.verify: The diet-group trajectories reproduce the paper's directional conclusion.
items.expected: The exclusive enteral nutrition group shows a drop in GMWI2 by day 2 that persists thereafter, whereas the Vegan and Omnivore groups stay near baseline without a clear sustained shift.

proof_flows.name: After broad-spectrum antibiotics, GMWI2 remains depressed long after alpha-diversity metrics begin to recover.

items.verify: Antibiotic-exposure time-course outputs exist for GMWI2, Shannon index, and species richness across sampling days.
items.expected: Usable per-timepoint outputs are present for GMWI2 and the two alpha-diversity comparators.

items.verify: The antibiotic longitudinal dataset structure matches the paper.
items.paper_reference: Results: For the third study30; Fig.6c
items.expected: The antibiotic study contains 12 healthy adults sampled at day 0, 4, 8, 42, and 180.

items.verify: The recovery pattern after antibiotics matches the paper.
items.expected: GMWI2 stays reduced relative to baseline through the last follow-up, while Shannon index and species richness show later partial recovery.

proof_flows.name: In vitro prebiotic fermentation increases GMWI2 for several oligosaccharides relative to no-substrate controls.

items.verify: A prebiotic-fermentation summary artifact exists with GMWI2 values for controls and oligosaccharide treatments.
items.expected: Usable group-level or replicate-level GMWI2 outputs are present for the fermentation experiment.

items.verify: The prebiotic experiment size and design match the paper.
items.paper_reference: Results: In the final study31; Fig.6d
items.expected: The experiment pools feces from 19 healthy adults and evaluates 7 study groups in triplicate.

items.verify: The ordering and significance pattern across prebiotic conditions matches the paper.
items.expected: The no-substrate 24 h control is lower than the no-substrate 0 h control; fructooligosaccharide, inulin, and galactooligosaccharide exceed the 0 h control; and those three plus xylooligosaccharide exceed the 24 h control, while 2'-fucosyllactose does not show the same improvement pattern.
